5.  In the graphâs **Visuals** tab, set [bars, lines, or points](#visuals) for the graph.
6.  Optionally:
    1.  In the **Alert** tab, configure an [alert and email notification](#alerts_config).

        Alerting isnât available in advanced mode.

    2.  In the **Advanced** tab, provide [advanced configurations](#advanced).

7.  Click **Apply Changes**.
8.  Exit the configuration view by clicking the **X** next to the configuration tabs.

## General Settings for Basic and Advanced Queries

**General** tab settings include [basic](#mode_basic) and [advanced](#mode_advanced) modes (or
queries) for configuring time-series data. Both configurations share fields for the title and a
short description.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Basic and Advanced Mode Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Title</strong></p></td><td><p>Title to display in the UI. Defaults to Panel Title.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Description</strong></p></td><td><p>Short description to display in the UI when users hover over the information (<code>i</code>) bubble.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Other settings are specific to the setting mode (basic or advanced).

### Basic Mode

In the **General** configuration tab, a basic query selects a single metric for a deployed Mule app
or API in a given environment.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Basic Mode Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Panel Type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>From the drop-down menu, select the type of panel to display for the resource (<strong>Application Panel</strong> or <strong>API Panel</strong>)</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Metric</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>The drop-down menu includes these types of metrics:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Inbound response metrics and averages</p></li><li><p>JVM metrics</p></li><li><p>Outbound response metrics and averages</p></li></ul></div><div><p>For a list of basic metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics">Basic Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Environment</strong></p></td><td><p>The environment where your application or API is deployed.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Resource</strong></p></td><td><p>A deployed application.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>Application Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>API</strong></p></td><td><p>A deployed API.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>API Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Version / Instance</strong></p></td><td><p>The Mule version or instance on which your API is deployed.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>API Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] When you create an application panel using a JVM metric, the query that supports the panel
> uses only the worker-id for the app. When you modify the panel at a later time, the resource
> fields are populated with the query content. Because some apps share a worker-id, the resource
> that appears in the selector might appear to be a different resource from the resource you
> selected originally. However, all resources share the same worker, so the query has the correct
> information despite the selector displaying a different resource name.

### Advanced Mode

In the **General** configuration tab, advanced mode settings support fine-tuned queries for metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT] If you switch back to basic mode, some advanced configuration queries are removed.

![Advanced configuration mode is on.](_images/config-general-advanced.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Advanced Mode Settings for Applications</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>From</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Lists metrics, such as <code>jvm.memory</code> and <code>app_inbound_metric</code>, that you can add to your chart.</p></div><div><p>This field provides matching suggestions when you type in a character. For example, <code>jvm.c</code> will produce a list of all values that contain those characters (such as <code>jvm.classloading</code> and <code>jvm.cpu.operatingsystem</code>).</p></div><div><p>For a list of advanced metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics_advanced">Advanced Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Where</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Supports conditional logic for filtering the <strong>From</strong> metric you selected.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Select one or more filters tags (such as <code>app_id</code>, <code>env_id</code>, <code>org_id</code>, <code>worker_id</code>). To avoid errors, you must provide required properties, such as the app or API, environment, and API version.</p></li><li><p>Available operators include:<br></p><div><ul><li><p><code>=</code> The value on the left matches the value on the right.</p></li><li><p><code>&lt;</code> The value on the left is less than the value on the right.</p></li><li><p><code>&gt;</code> The value on the left is greater than the value on the right.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p>You can also construct a query that contains multiple filtering expressions by using logical <code>AND</code> or <code>OR</code> operators between the expressions.</p><div><p>Example:</p></div></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox</code></p></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox AND app_id= app3</code></p></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox OR env_id = Production</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Grouping <code>AND</code> or <code>OR</code> conditions within parentheses is <em>not allowed</em>. For example, <code>Where env_id = Sandbox AND (app_id= app3 OR app_id=app2)</code> isnât allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Select</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Add one or more metric <code>field</code> settings to create a function that selects a field from the selected metric value in <strong>From</strong> and applies an operation to it (such as the <code>mean</code> of the <code>heap_total</code>).</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group By</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For grouping the data by a selected time interval (such as <code>$_interval</code>, <code>1s</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>1h</code>), filter tag (such as <code>tag(env_id)</code>), and fill options (such as <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>).</p></div><div><p>Valid values and arguments:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>time($_interval)</code> for the automated time interval setting, or you can specify time intervals of <code>1m</code>, <code>5m</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>15m</code>, or <code>1h</code>.</p><div><ul><li><p>To avoid service timeouts when querying over long time ranges, consider using a time interval setting.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p><code>fill()</code> where valid values are <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>LIMIT</code>: Numeric value (N) used to limit the result to the first N data points (combined series and timestamp values) returned by a query.</p></li><li><p><code>SLIMIT</code>: Numeric value (N) used to return all data points (combined series and timestamp values) from the first N series returned by the query.</p></li><li><p><code>ORDER BY time DESC</code>: Returns results in descending time order.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Alias By</strong></p></td><td><p>You can create a label for your metric using a literal string (such as <code>my string</code>) or supported variables (for example, <code>$tag_worker_id</code> or <code>$tag_env_id</code>).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Advanced Mode Settings for APIs</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>From</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Lists metrics, such as <code>http_summary_metric</code>, that you can add to your chart.</p></div><div><p>For a list of advanced metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics_advanced">Advanced Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Where</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Provides conditional logic for filtering the metric selected from the <strong>From</strong> field.</p></div><div><p>You can select one or more filter tags (such as <code>api_id</code>, <code>org_id</code>, <code>api_version_id</code>, <code>method</code>, <code>status code</code>). Example: <code>Where status_code = 200</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Select</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For creating a function that selects a metric field (such as <code>request_size_average</code>,<code>response_time_max</code>, or <code>response_time_sum</code>) from the metric selected in the <strong>From</strong> field and applies an operation to it (such as the <code>mean</code> of the <code>response_time_average</code>). You can add one or more metric <code>field</code> settings.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group by</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For grouping the data by a selected time interval (such as <code>$_interval</code>, <code>1s</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>1h</code>), filter tag (such as <code>tag(api_id)</code>), and fill options (such as <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>).</p></div><div><ul><li><p>To avoid service timeouts when querying over long time ranges, consider using a time interval setting.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Alias by</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>You can create a label for your metric using a literal string (such as <code>my string</code>) or supported variables (for example, <code>$tag_api_id</code>). The alias must always begin with <code>$tag</code>. The syntax is <code>$tag_yourTagName</code>.</p></div><div><p>To use your tag as an alias, the tag must be included in the <code>group by</code> field of the query.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Axes (X-Axis and Y-Axis Settings)

Configure the x-axis and y-axis of your graph in the **Axes** tab.

### Y-Axis Settings

Set a vertical y-axis for the left (Left Y) or right (Right Y) side of your graph.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Y-Axis Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Show</strong></p></td><td><p>If unchecked, hides the y-axis from the graph.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Unit</strong></p></td><td><p>The unit of measurement to use for values on the y-axis. Values include <code>none</code>, data types (such as <code>short</code>, <code>percent (1-100)</code>), <code>currency</code> settings (including <code>Dollars $</code> or <code>Real ($)</code>), <code>time</code>, <code>data</code>, <code>data rate</code>, and many other types of values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Scale</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Valid values for a linear or logarithmic (log) scale for coordinates on the y-axis:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>linear</strong>: For y-coordinates, such as <code>1</code>,<code>2</code>,<code>3</code>.</p></li><li><p><strong>log (base 2)</strong>: For y-coordinates, such as <code>1</code>,<code>4</code>,<code>16</code>.</p></li><li><p><strong>log (base 10)</strong>: For y-coordinates, such as <code>1</code>,<code>10</code>,<code>100</code>.</p></li><li><p><strong>log (base 32)</strong>: For y-coordinates, such as <code>1</code>,<code>32</code>,<code>1024</code>.</p></li><li><p><strong>log (base 1024)</strong>: For y-coordinates, such as <code>1</code>,<code>1024</code>,<code>1048576</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Y-Min</strong></p></td><td><p>The minimum value for a y-coordinate. Defaults to <code>auto</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Y-Max</strong></p></td><td><p>The maximum value for a y-coordinate. Defaults to <code>auto</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Decimals</strong></p></td><td><p>Overrides the automatic decimal precision on y-coordinates, for example, where <code>2</code> limits a value of <code>10.12345</code> to <code>10.12</code>. Defaults to <code>auto</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Label</strong></p></td><td><p>A string used to label the Y axis. Defaults to <code>""</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### X-Axis Settings

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">X-Axis Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Show</strong></p></td><td><p>If unchecked, hides the x-axis from the graph.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Mode</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Valid values:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Time</strong>: The default option. Indicates that x-axis data is divided into time units (for example, hours or minutes).</p></li><li><p><strong>Series</strong>: Indicates that data is divided by series, and the y-axis provides the value of the series data.</p></li><li><p><strong>Histogram</strong>: Converts the graph into a bar chart that represents collections of data at specified, numeric intervals (called a buckets or bins). The higher a bar in a histogram, the greater the frequency (or count) of data collected at that interval.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Value</strong></p></td><td><p>Visible only if <strong>Series</strong> mode is selected.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Buckets</strong></p></td><td><p>Visible only if <strong>Histogram</strong> mode is selected. Divides the range of values for a given metric into the specified number of intervals so that the histogram can display the count or frequency of data points that fall into that interval.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

After you complete your configuration, you can click Save Changes. To exit the configuration and
return to the dashboard, simply click the `X`.

## Visuals (Bar, Line, Data Point Settings)

You can configure the style and look of graph to use. Bar, line, and point graphs are supported.

![Singlestat example](_images/graph-config-visuals.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Visual Configuration Options</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Draw Modes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>You can check one or more of these modes:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Bars</strong>: Checkbox for representing values as bar charts.</p></li><li><p><strong>Lines</strong>: Checkbox for representing values as line graphs.</p></li><li><p><strong>Points</strong>: Checkbox for representing values as data points.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Mode Options</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Only available when the selected <strong>Draw Modes</strong> are <strong>Lines</strong> or <strong>Points</strong>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Point Radius</strong>: Only available when <strong>Points</strong> is selected from <strong>Draw Modes</strong>. On a scale of <code>0</code> (none) to <code>10</code>, sets the circumference of the line that connects related data points on the graph.</p></li><li><p><strong>Fill</strong>: Only available when <strong>Lines</strong> is selected from <strong>Draw Modes</strong>. On a scale of <code>0</code> (none) to <code>10</code>, sets the opacity of the color used below related data points on the graph.</p></li><li><p><strong>Line Width</strong>: Only available when <strong>Lines</strong> is selected from <strong>Draw Modes</strong>. On a scale of <code>0</code> (none) to <code>10</code>, sets the thickness of the border of the fill.</p></li><li><p><strong>Staircase</strong>: Checkbox for displaying adjacent points in a staircase pattern.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Hide Series</strong></p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><strong>With Nulls Only</strong>: Checkbox for hiding any series that is filled with null values.</p></li><li><p><strong>With Only Zeros</strong>: Checkbox for hiding any series that is filled with values of zero.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Alerts (Configuration, Notifications, History)

<table><colgroup><col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/info.png" alt="info" width="15" height="15" title="This feature requires an Advanced or Titanium subscription."></span> Availability: <a href="./#billing">Advanced and Titanium tiers only</a></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

From a graphâs **Alerts** tab, you can:

- [Create an alert](#create)
- [Configure an alert](#configure)
- [View alert history](#state_history)
- [Delete an alert](#delete_alert)

### Create an Alert for a Chart in a Custom Dashboard

1.  Navigate to the configuration screen for a graph in a custom dashboard. For guidance, see
    [Configuring Custom Dashboards](dashboard-custom-config).
2.  Provide a general configuration for the graph that (at minimum) defines the metric, environment,
    and resource for which you want to set the alert.

    For guidance, see [General Settings](#general_settings).

3.  Navigate to the **Alert** configuration tab for the graph.
4.  Ensure that **Advanced Mode** is disabled.

    Alerts arenât available for charts with advanced mode configurations.

5.  Click **Add Alert**.
6.  Use **Alert Config** to configure the alert. See [Alert Config](#alert_config).
7.  Configure email notifications when an alert is triggered. See [Notifications](#notifications).
8.  Click **Save Changes**.
9.  Click `X` to exit the configuration and return to the dashboard.

Note that you can also view [state history](#state_history) and [delete](#delete_alert) alerts.

### Configure an Alert for a Chart in a Custom Dashboard

In the **Alert Config** section, configure the condition under which an alert is triggered.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Settings for Alerts in Custom Dashboards</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Name</p></td><td><p>A title for your alert</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Severity level</p></td><td><p>The severity level of your alert.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>Condition</code></p></td><td><p>WHEN: For triggering an alert when the metric being measured is above a specified level</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Available severity levels:

Info

May be assigned to alerts that do not require immediate attention when triggered. This severity
indicates the metric should be passively monitored.

Warning

May be assigned to alerts that require prompt attention when triggered. This severity indicates an
alert should be closely monitored.

Critical

May be assigned to alerts that require immediate attention when triggered. This severity indicates
an alert should receive an immediate response.

### Send Notifications

Configure an email notification when an alert is triggered.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Notification Settings for Alerts</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>Send To</code></p></td><td><p>Email address for each recipient of the alert notification. If you add or change the email address of an alert recipient, they donât receive an email until the alert is triggered.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>Message</code></p></td><td><p>Message to send to the recipient.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Get State History

View the last fifty state changes in this section of the UI.

You can also clear the history.

### Delete an Alert

To delete an alert from a chart in a custom dashboard, click **Delete** from the chartâs **Alerts**
tab, and confirm your deletion.

## Advanced (Legends, Thresholds, Stacking, Hovering, Time Shift Settings)

Add and configure legends, thresholds, and other features for your graph.

### Legends (Advanced Setting)

In the **Advanced configuration** tab, configure legends for your graphs.

![Advanced graph settings for a legend](_images/graph-advanced-legend.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2"><strong>Legend Options</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Show</strong></p></td><td><p>Unchecking hides the legend. Checked by default.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>As Table</strong></p></td><td><p>Check to display the selected legend Values (such as <code>min</code> and <code>max</code>) in a table. Unchecked by default.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>To the Right</strong></p></td><td><p>Check to display the legend on the right side. Unchecked by default.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Width</strong></p></td><td><p>Available when <strong>To the Right</strong> is checked. Sets the minimum width (in pixels) for the legend. Leave it blank to allow for automatic width selection.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

You can also display the following values in a legend:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2"><strong>Legend Values</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Min</p></td><td><p>Minimum of all values returned from the query</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Max</p></td><td><p>Maximum of all values returned from the query</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Avg</p></td><td><p>Average of all values returned from the query</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Current</p></td><td><p>Last value returned from the query</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Total</p></td><td><p>Sum of all values returned from metric query</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Decimals</p></td><td><p>Overrides automatic decimal precision used in legends and tooltips</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

After you complete your configuration, click **Save Changes**.

To exit the configuration and return to the dashboard, click the **X**.

### Stacking and Hover (Advanced Setting)

In the **Advanced configuration** tab, you can configure a hover tooltip to display information
about a data point in a graph when you hover over the value.

![Advanced graph settings, Stack and Hover](_images/graph-advanced-stacking-hover.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Hover Tooltip Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Mode</strong></p></td><td><p>To display one or all series in the tooltip.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Sort Order</strong></p></td><td><p>Displays data in no order (None), Decreasing, or Increasing order. Defaults to None.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Stacked Value</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Available when <strong>Stack</strong> is checked. Controls the display of values in the tooltip.</p></div><div><p>Valid values are:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Individual</strong>: The value of the series that you hover over.</p></li><li><p><strong>Cumulative</strong>: Sum of series below the stacked value plus the series you hover over.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

Stacking and Null functionality displays multiple series as a group within a stack. You can also
specify how to deal with null values.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2"><strong>Stacking and Null Settings</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Stack</strong></p></td><td><p>When checked, stacks each series, one on top of another, instead of overlaying them.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Percent</strong></p></td><td><p>Available when <strong>Stack</strong> is checked. Displays each series of stacked values as a percentage.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Null Value</strong></p></td><td><p>Determines how to display null values: Connected, Null, Null As Zero.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Threshold (Advanced Setting)

In the **Advanced** configuration tab, you can set one or more thresholds, such as a line or section
to a graph, to indicate whether the data crosses a limit that you specify.

> [!NOTE] Thresholds are not available when an alert is in place on the graph. To re-enable
> thresholds for a chart, you must delete the alert.

![Threshold settings for a graph](_images/graph-advanced-threshold.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Threshold Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>gt</strong> or <strong>lt</strong></p></td><td><p>Indicates whether the limit applies to data that is greater than (<code>gt</code>) or less than (<code>lt</code>) the specified threshold value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Threshold value</p></td><td><p>A numeric value for the threshold.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Threshold type</p></td><td><p>Identifier for the threshold (Custom, Critical, Warning, or OK).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fill</strong></p></td><td><p>Adds a filler color to the graph that corresponds to the specified threshold setting.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Line</strong></p></td><td><p>Adds a line to the graph that corresponds to the specified threshold setting.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Fill Color</p></td><td><p>Similar to <strong>Fill</strong> settings, but for custom thresholds only.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Line Color</p></td><td><p>Similar to <strong>Line</strong> settings, but for custom thresholds only.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Time Range Shift (Advanced Setting)

In the **Advanced** configuration tab, you can override any time intervals or ranges that end users
select for the custom dashboard. Such selections are global and apply to all charts in the
dashboard. Overrides are useful when the selection does not make sense for a particular graph.

Time ranges are calculated backwards from the present time. For example, you might set the time
range so you can view the last 2 hours of data in a graph. You can also shift the time range to
calculate backwards from an earlier point in time, for example, from 1 hour ago instead of from the
present time.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2"><strong>Time Range Shift Settings</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Override Relative Time</strong></p></td><td><p>The amount of time to show in the x-axis of the graph (for example, <code>2h</code>). Information about this setting is displayed under the title of the graph unless you select Hide Time Override Info.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Add Time Shift</strong></p></td><td><p>Amount of time before now (the current time) to <em>end</em> (or shift back in time) the duration set by Override Relative Time. For example, if the time right now is 10:00, and you shift the time to <code>1h</code> when the Override Relative Time is <code>2h</code>, end users will see the two hours of data available from 7:00 to 9:00, and the table will also display a message like this: <code>2 hours, ending 1 hour ago</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Hide Time Override Info</strong></p></td><td><p>Checkbox for hiding the description of any Override Relative Time and Time Shift settings from the graph.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Configuring Singlestats in a Custom Dashboard

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

[![](../_/img/icons/info.svg)](/monitoring/#billing)

Configure singlestat charts for Mule apps and APIs in an Anypoint Monitoring custom dashboard. A
singlestat chart displays a single value, such as the value of an Inbound - Average Request Count by
Endpoint metric.

![Singlestat chart and configuration view](_images/dashboard-custom-config-singlestat.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>A singlestat chart example<div><p>You can customize the look of singlestats.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Singlestat configuration tabs:<div><ul><li><p><a href="#general"><strong>General</strong></a>: Singlestat title, description, and mode (basic or advanced)</p></li><li><p><a href="#time-range"><strong>Time range</strong></a>: Override settings for relative time ranges selected from the dashboard UI by end users</p></li><li><p><a href="#options"><strong>Options</strong></a>: Style settings</p></li><li><p><a href="#value-mappings"><strong>Value Mappings</strong></a>: Human-readable label settings</p></li><li><p><strong>X</strong> for closing the edit view</p></li></ul></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><strong>Advanced Mode</strong>: Switch for enabling <a href="#mode_advanced">advanced</a> configurations</td></tr></tbody></table>

## Create a Singlestat Chart

Create a singlestat chart in a custom dashboard by following the guidance in
[Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#charts_adding).

## Configure a Singlestat Chart

Configure a singlestat chart in a custom dashboard.

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open the custom dashboard that contains your singlestat chart.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#view).

2.  Click the singlestatâs ![](_images/more-menu-vert-ellipsis.png) (**More menu**), and select
    **Configure**.
3.  Click the singlestatâs **General** configuration tab.
    1.  Provide a title and description.
    2.  Select and configure a singlestat mode (basic or advanced).

        Both modes require environment, resource (app or API), and metric settings. For more
        information, see [General Settings for Basic and Advanced Queries](#general).

4.  Optionally:
    1.  In the singlestatâs [**Time range**](#time-range) tab, set the singlestatâs overrides for
        the time range selected by users.
    2.  In the [**Options**](#options) tab, configure singlestat fonts, coloring, spark lines, and
        other style settings.
    3.  In the [**Value Mappings**](#value-mappings) tab, provide a human-readable label for a
        summary statistic.

5.  Click **Apply Changes**.
6.  Exit the configuration view by clicking the **X** next to the configuration tabs.

## General Settings for Basic and Advanced Queries

**General** tab settings include [basic](#mode_basic) and [advanced](#mode_advanced) modes (or
queries) for configuring time-series data. Both configurations share fields for the title and a
short description.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Basic and Advanced Mode Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Title</strong></p></td><td><p>Title to display in the UI. Defaults to Panel Title.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Description</strong></p></td><td><p>Short description to display in the UI when users hover over the information (<code>i</code>) bubble.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Other settings are specific to the setting mode (basic or advanced).

### Basic Mode

In the **General** configuration tab, a basic query selects a single metric for a deployed Mule app
or API in a given environment.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Basic Mode Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Panel Type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>From the drop-down menu, select the type of panel to display for the resource (<strong>Application Panel</strong> or <strong>API Panel</strong>)</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Metric</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>The drop-down menu includes these types of metrics:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Inbound response metrics and averages</p></li><li><p>JVM metrics</p></li><li><p>Outbound response metrics and averages</p></li></ul></div><div><p>For a list of basic metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics">Basic Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Environment</strong></p></td><td><p>The environment where your application or API is deployed.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Resource</strong></p></td><td><p>A deployed application.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>Application Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>API</strong></p></td><td><p>A deployed API.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>API Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Version / Instance</strong></p></td><td><p>The Mule version or instance on which your API is deployed.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>API Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] When you create an application panel using a JVM metric, the query that supports the panel
> uses only the worker-id for the app. When you modify the panel at a later time, the resource
> fields are populated with the query content. Because some apps share a worker-id, the resource
> that appears in the selector might appear to be a different resource from the resource you
> selected originally. However, all resources share the same worker, so the query has the correct
> information despite the selector displaying a different resource name.

### Advanced Mode

In the **General** configuration tab, advanced mode settings support fine-tuned queries for metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT] If you switch back to basic mode, some advanced configuration queries are removed.

![Advanced configuration mode is on.](_images/config-general-advanced.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Advanced Mode Settings for Applications</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>From</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Lists metrics, such as <code>jvm.memory</code> and <code>app_inbound_metric</code>, that you can add to your chart.</p></div><div><p>This field provides matching suggestions when you type in a character. For example, <code>jvm.c</code> will produce a list of all values that contain those characters (such as <code>jvm.classloading</code> and <code>jvm.cpu.operatingsystem</code>).</p></div><div><p>For a list of advanced metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics_advanced">Advanced Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Where</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Supports conditional logic for filtering the <strong>From</strong> metric you selected.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Select one or more filters tags (such as <code>app_id</code>, <code>env_id</code>, <code>org_id</code>, <code>worker_id</code>). To avoid errors, you must provide required properties, such as the app or API, environment, and API version.</p></li><li><p>Available operators include:<br></p><div><ul><li><p><code>=</code> The value on the left matches the value on the right.</p></li><li><p><code>&lt;</code> The value on the left is less than the value on the right.</p></li><li><p><code>&gt;</code> The value on the left is greater than the value on the right.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p>You can also construct a query that contains multiple filtering expressions by using logical <code>AND</code> or <code>OR</code> operators between the expressions.</p><div><p>Example:</p></div></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox</code></p></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox AND app_id= app3</code></p></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox OR env_id = Production</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Grouping <code>AND</code> or <code>OR</code> conditions within parentheses is <em>not allowed</em>. For example, <code>Where env_id = Sandbox AND (app_id= app3 OR app_id=app2)</code> isnât allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Select</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Add one or more metric <code>field</code> settings to create a function that selects a field from the selected metric value in <strong>From</strong> and applies an operation to it (such as the <code>mean</code> of the <code>heap_total</code>).</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group By</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For grouping the data by a selected time interval (such as <code>$_interval</code>, <code>1s</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>1h</code>), filter tag (such as <code>tag(env_id)</code>), and fill options (such as <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>).</p></div><div><p>Valid values and arguments:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>time($_interval)</code> for the automated time interval setting, or you can specify time intervals of <code>1m</code>, <code>5m</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>15m</code>, or <code>1h</code>.</p><div><ul><li><p>To avoid service timeouts when querying over long time ranges, consider using a time interval setting.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p><code>fill()</code> where valid values are <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>LIMIT</code>: Numeric value (N) used to limit the result to the first N data points (combined series and timestamp values) returned by a query.</p></li><li><p><code>SLIMIT</code>: Numeric value (N) used to return all data points (combined series and timestamp values) from the first N series returned by the query.</p></li><li><p><code>ORDER BY time DESC</code>: Returns results in descending time order.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Alias By</strong></p></td><td><p>You can create a label for your metric using a literal string (such as <code>my string</code>) or supported variables (for example, <code>$tag_worker_id</code> or <code>$tag_env_id</code>).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Advanced Mode Settings for APIs</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>From</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Lists metrics, such as <code>http_summary_metric</code>, that you can add to your chart.</p></div><div><p>For a list of advanced metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics_advanced">Advanced Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Where</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Provides conditional logic for filtering the metric selected from the <strong>From</strong> field.</p></div><div><p>You can select one or more filter tags (such as <code>api_id</code>, <code>org_id</code>, <code>api_version_id</code>, <code>method</code>, <code>status code</code>). Example: <code>Where status_code = 200</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Select</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For creating a function that selects a metric field (such as <code>request_size_average</code>,<code>response_time_max</code>, or <code>response_time_sum</code>) from the metric selected in the <strong>From</strong> field and applies an operation to it (such as the <code>mean</code> of the <code>response_time_average</code>). You can add one or more metric <code>field</code> settings.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group by</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For grouping the data by a selected time interval (such as <code>$_interval</code>, <code>1s</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>1h</code>), filter tag (such as <code>tag(api_id)</code>), and fill options (such as <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>).</p></div><div><ul><li><p>To avoid service timeouts when querying over long time ranges, consider using a time interval setting.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Alias by</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>You can create a label for your metric using a literal string (such as <code>my string</code>) or supported variables (for example, <code>$tag_api_id</code>). The alias must always begin with <code>$tag</code>. The syntax is <code>$tag_yourTagName</code>.</p></div><div><p>To use your tag as an alias, the tag must be included in the <code>group by</code> field of the query.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Time Range (Overrides, Time Shifts, Info Display)

Time range configurations provide a way to override _relative_ time ranges (such as the last 30
minutes) selected from the dashboard UI by end users.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Time Range Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Override Relative Time</strong></p></td><td><p>The amount of time covered by the statistic, for example, the last hour (<code>1h</code>) or last thirty minutes (<code>30m</code>). This setting overrides only <em>relative</em> time ranges selected by end users. When a user selects an <em>absolute time and date range</em> for the dashboard, the override is disabled.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Add Time Shift</strong></p></td><td><p>How long ago the covered time range ended, for example, <code>1s</code> for one second ago.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Hide Time Override Info</strong></p></td><td><p>Hides information about the override time range settings from the singlestat or table, such as <code>30 minutes, ending 1 second ago</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Options Tab

The **Options** tab includes settings for singlestat [values](#value_singlestat), fonts,
[coloring](#color_singlestat), [spark lines](#spark_singlestat), and [gauge icon](#gauge_singlestat)
settings.

### Value Settings for Singlestats

Configure values for your singlestat. Value settings include the type of value you want (such as an
average), the unit of measure, and others.

![Singlestat options example](_images/singlestat-config-options.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Values</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Stat</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>The value displayed by the singlestat. Available Settings:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Min</strong>: Minimum of recorded values for the specified metric.</p></li><li><p><strong>Max</strong>: Maximum of recorded values for the specified metric.</p></li><li><p><strong>Average</strong>: Average of the recorded values for the specified metric.</p></li><li><p><strong>Current</strong>: The current value for the specified metric.</p></li><li><p><strong>Total</strong>: Sum of recorded values for the specified metric.</p></li><li><p><strong>Name</strong>: Name of the metric (for example, <code>worker-0</code>).</p></li><li><p><strong>First</strong>: The first recorded value for the specified metric.</p></li><li><p><strong>Delta</strong>: The total incremental increase in the series.</p></li><li><p><strong>Difference</strong>: The difference between the Current value (last value) and the First value.</p></li><li><p><strong>Range</strong>: The difference between Min and Max.</p></li><li><p><strong>Time of Last Point</strong>: Timestamp of the last reading.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Prefix</strong></p></td><td><p>Prepends text to the singlestat value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Postfix</strong></p></td><td><p>Appends text to the singlestat value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Unit</strong></p></td><td><p>Unit of measurement to display with the singlestat value (for example, <code>percent (0-100)</code>).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Decimals</strong></p></td><td><p>Overrides the automatic decimal precision for the legend and tooltips.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Font size</strong></p></td><td><p>Select the font size for the stat, prefix, and postfix.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Coloring and Thresholds

Add color-coded thresholds to a singlestat so that you can see whether it exceeds or stays within
expected limits.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Coloring</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Background</strong></p></td><td><p>Adds a background color determined by the <strong>Thresholds</strong> setting.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Value</p></td><td><p>Applies a color for the current singlestat value that matches the color determined by the <strong>Thresholds</strong> setting.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Thresholds</strong></p></td><td><p>Sets a threshold using a numeric range such as <code>10,20</code> to distinguish values between the two numbers, below left-most number, and above the right-most number.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Colors</strong></p></td><td><p>Sets the colors for the values that are below, within, or above the <strong>Thresholds</strong> settings. You can invert the colors, as needed.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Spark Lines

Configure spark line settings to show a visual representation of the data.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Spark Lines</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Show</strong></p></td><td><p>Checkbox to show or hide the spark line.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Full Height</strong></p></td><td><p>For covering the full height of the singlestat.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Line Color</strong></p></td><td><p>For selecting the color of the spark line.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fill Color</strong></p></td><td><p>For selecting the color of the area below the spark line.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Gauge

Display an arc-like gauge that represents the value of the singlestat.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Gauge</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Show</strong></p></td><td><p>Checkbox for showing or hiding the gauge.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Min</strong></p></td><td><p>Sets a minimum value (such as <code>0</code> for a gauge that shows a percentage).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Max</strong></p></td><td><p>Sets a maximum value (such as <code>100</code> for a gauge that shows a percentage).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Threshold Labels</strong></p></td><td><p>Checkbox for showing or hiding your Threshold settings.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Value Mappings

From the **Value Mappings** configuration tab, you can provide a human-readable label for a summary
statistic.

![Value mapping example](_images/singlestat-config-value-mapping.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Range to Text Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Value to Text</strong></p></td><td><p>Maps a number to a textual description, for example: <code>0</code> â <code>Error</code>, <code>1</code> â <code>Success</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Range to Text</strong></p></td><td><p>Maps a numeric range to a textual description, for example: From <code>5</code> To <code>10</code> Text <code>Warning</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Configuring Tables in a Custom Dashboard

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

[![](../_/img/icons/info.svg)](/monitoring/#billing)

Configure tables with Mule app and API metrics in custom dashboards.

![Table chart and configuration view](_images/dashboard-custom-config-table.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>A table configured with multiple queries in advanced mode</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Table configuration tabs:<div><ul><li><p><a href="#general"><strong>General</strong></a>: Table title, description, and mode (basic or advanced)</p></li><li><p><a href="#time-range"><strong>Time range</strong></a>: Override settings for relative time ranges selected from the dashboard UI by end users</p></li><li><p><a href="#options"><strong>Options</strong></a>: Table layout, column, and pagination options</p></li><li><p><a href="#column-styles"><strong>Column Styles</strong></a>: Rules for table columns</p></li><li><p><strong>X</strong> for closing the edit view</p></li></ul></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><strong>Advanced Mode</strong>: Switch for enabling <a href="#mode_advanced">advanced</a> configuration mode<div><p>Advanced mode support complex configurations with up to 25 queries.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td>Icons for moving, duplicating, and deleting queries from an advanced mode configuration</td></tr></tbody></table>

## Create a Table

Create a table in a custom dashboard by following the guidance in
[Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#charts_adding).

## Configure a Table

Configure a table in a custom dashboard.

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open the custom dashboard that contains your table.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#view).

2.  Click the tableâs ![](_images/more-menu-vert-ellipsis.png) (**More menu**), and select
    **Configure**.
3.  Click the tableâs **General** configuration tab.
    1.  Provide a title and description.
    2.  Select and configure a table mode (basic or advanced).

        Both modes require environment, resource (app or API), and metric settings. For more
        information, see [General Settings for Basic and Advanced Queries](#general).

4.  Optionally:
    1.  In the tableâs [**Time range**](#time-range) tab, set the tableâs overrides for the time
        range selected by users.
    2.  In the [**Options**](#options) tab, configure table layout, column, and pagination options.
    3.  In the [**Column Styles**](#column-styles) tab, set rules for table columns.

5.  Click **Apply Changes**.
6.  Exit the configuration view by clicking the **X** next to the configuration tabs.

## General Settings for Basic and Advanced Queries

**General** tab settings include [basic](#mode_basic) and [advanced](#mode_advanced) modes (or
queries) for configuring time-series data. Both configurations share fields for the title and a
short description.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Basic and Advanced Mode Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Title</strong></p></td><td><p>Title to display in the UI. Defaults to Panel Title.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Description</strong></p></td><td><p>Short description to display in the UI when users hover over the information (<code>i</code>) bubble.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Other settings are specific to the setting mode (basic or advanced).

### Basic Mode

In the **General** configuration tab, a basic query selects a single metric for a deployed Mule app
or API in a given environment.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Basic Mode Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Panel Type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>From the drop-down menu, select the type of panel to display for the resource (<strong>Application Panel</strong> or <strong>API Panel</strong>)</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Metric</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>The drop-down menu includes these types of metrics:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Inbound response metrics and averages</p></li><li><p>JVM metrics</p></li><li><p>Outbound response metrics and averages</p></li></ul></div><div><p>For a list of basic metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics">Basic Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Environment</strong></p></td><td><p>The environment where your application or API is deployed.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Resource</strong></p></td><td><p>A deployed application.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>Application Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>API</strong></p></td><td><p>A deployed API.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>API Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Version / Instance</strong></p></td><td><p>The Mule version or instance on which your API is deployed.</p><p>This option is available if you select <strong>API Panel</strong> as your panel type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] When you create an application panel using a JVM metric, the query that supports the panel
> uses only the worker-id for the app. When you modify the panel at a later time, the resource
> fields are populated with the query content. Because some apps share a worker-id, the resource
> that appears in the selector might appear to be a different resource from the resource you
> selected originally. However, all resources share the same worker, so the query has the correct
> information despite the selector displaying a different resource name.

### Advanced Mode

In the **General** configuration tab, advanced mode settings support fine-tuned queries for metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT] If you switch back to basic mode, some advanced configuration queries are removed.

![Advanced configuration mode is on.](_images/config-general-advanced.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Advanced Mode Settings for Applications</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>From</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Lists metrics, such as <code>jvm.memory</code> and <code>app_inbound_metric</code>, that you can add to your chart.</p></div><div><p>This field provides matching suggestions when you type in a character. For example, <code>jvm.c</code> will produce a list of all values that contain those characters (such as <code>jvm.classloading</code> and <code>jvm.cpu.operatingsystem</code>).</p></div><div><p>For a list of advanced metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics_advanced">Advanced Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Where</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Supports conditional logic for filtering the <strong>From</strong> metric you selected.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Select one or more filters tags (such as <code>app_id</code>, <code>env_id</code>, <code>org_id</code>, <code>worker_id</code>). To avoid errors, you must provide required properties, such as the app or API, environment, and API version.</p></li><li><p>Available operators include:<br></p><div><ul><li><p><code>=</code> The value on the left matches the value on the right.</p></li><li><p><code>&lt;</code> The value on the left is less than the value on the right.</p></li><li><p><code>&gt;</code> The value on the left is greater than the value on the right.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p>You can also construct a query that contains multiple filtering expressions by using logical <code>AND</code> or <code>OR</code> operators between the expressions.</p><div><p>Example:</p></div></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox</code></p></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox AND app_id= app3</code></p></li><li><p><code>Where env_id = Sandbox OR env_id = Production</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Grouping <code>AND</code> or <code>OR</code> conditions within parentheses is <em>not allowed</em>. For example, <code>Where env_id = Sandbox AND (app_id= app3 OR app_id=app2)</code> isnât allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Select</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Add one or more metric <code>field</code> settings to create a function that selects a field from the selected metric value in <strong>From</strong> and applies an operation to it (such as the <code>mean</code> of the <code>heap_total</code>).</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group By</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For grouping the data by a selected time interval (such as <code>$_interval</code>, <code>1s</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>1h</code>), filter tag (such as <code>tag(env_id)</code>), and fill options (such as <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>).</p></div><div><p>Valid values and arguments:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>time($_interval)</code> for the automated time interval setting, or you can specify time intervals of <code>1m</code>, <code>5m</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>15m</code>, or <code>1h</code>.</p><div><ul><li><p>To avoid service timeouts when querying over long time ranges, consider using a time interval setting.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p><code>fill()</code> where valid values are <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>LIMIT</code>: Numeric value (N) used to limit the result to the first N data points (combined series and timestamp values) returned by a query.</p></li><li><p><code>SLIMIT</code>: Numeric value (N) used to return all data points (combined series and timestamp values) from the first N series returned by the query.</p></li><li><p><code>ORDER BY time DESC</code>: Returns results in descending time order.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Alias By</strong></p></td><td><p>You can create a label for your metric using a literal string (such as <code>my string</code>) or supported variables (for example, <code>$tag_worker_id</code> or <code>$tag_env_id</code>).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Advanced Mode Settings for APIs</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>From</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Lists metrics, such as <code>http_summary_metric</code>, that you can add to your chart.</p></div><div><p>For a list of advanced metrics, see <a href="dashboard-config-ref#metrics_advanced">Advanced Mode Metrics</a></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Where</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Provides conditional logic for filtering the metric selected from the <strong>From</strong> field.</p></div><div><p>You can select one or more filter tags (such as <code>api_id</code>, <code>org_id</code>, <code>api_version_id</code>, <code>method</code>, <code>status code</code>). Example: <code>Where status_code = 200</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Select</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For creating a function that selects a metric field (such as <code>request_size_average</code>,<code>response_time_max</code>, or <code>response_time_sum</code>) from the metric selected in the <strong>From</strong> field and applies an operation to it (such as the <code>mean</code> of the <code>response_time_average</code>). You can add one or more metric <code>field</code> settings.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group by</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For grouping the data by a selected time interval (such as <code>$_interval</code>, <code>1s</code>, <code>10m</code>, <code>1h</code>), filter tag (such as <code>tag(api_id)</code>), and fill options (such as <code>none</code>, <code>null</code>, <code>0</code>, <code>previous</code>, <code>linear</code>).</p></div><div><ul><li><p>To avoid service timeouts when querying over long time ranges, consider using a time interval setting.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Alias by</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>You can create a label for your metric using a literal string (such as <code>my string</code>) or supported variables (for example, <code>$tag_api_id</code>). The alias must always begin with <code>$tag</code>. The syntax is <code>$tag_yourTagName</code>.</p></div><div><p>To use your tag as an alias, the tag must be included in the <code>group by</code> field of the query.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Time Range (Overrides, Time Shifts, Info Display)

Time range configurations provide a way to override _relative_ time ranges (such as the last 30
minutes) selected from the dashboard UI by end users.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Time Range Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Override Relative Time</strong></p></td><td><p>The amount of time covered by the statistic, for example, the last hour (<code>1h</code>) or last thirty minutes (<code>30m</code>). This setting overrides only <em>relative</em> time ranges selected by end users. When a user selects an <em>absolute time and date range</em> for the dashboard, the override is disabled.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Add Time Shift</strong></p></td><td><p>How long ago the covered time range ended, for example, <code>1s</code> for one second ago.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Hide Time Override Info</strong></p></td><td><p>Hides information about the override time range settings from the singlestat or table, such as <code>30 minutes, ending 1 second ago</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Options (Data and Pagination Settings)

In the **Options** configuration tab, you can set layout, column, and pagination options for tables
in a custom dashboard.

### Data Settings (Options tab)

Provide time series data transformation, column, and paging configurations.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Value Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Time Series to Rows</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Creates a table with values for a given metric at a given time period. Like Time Series to Columns, the first column, Time, is for timestamps (such as <code>2018-04-25 06:37:40</code>). However, subsequent columns are Metric (for listing specific metrics, such as <code>worker-0</code>) and Value for listing the values of the metric at that time period.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time Series to Columns</p></td><td><div><div><p>Creates a table that shows values for a given metric at a given time period. Like Time Series to Rows, the first column, Time, is for timestamps. However, there is a column for each metric, such as <code>worker-0</code>, which lists the values of that metric at a given time period.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time Series to Aggregations</p></td><td><div><div><p>Transforms time series into a table that calculates totals, averages, and so on for a given metric. The first column, Metric, is for metrics such as <code>worker-0</code>, and subsequent columns are for any combination of these values: Avg, Min, Max, Total, Current, or Count for a given metric.</p></div><div><p>Note that this example uses color-coded <a href="#thresholds">thresholds</a> (see the cell coloring in the Avg and Max cells and value coloring for the Min value).</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

If you select Time Series to Aggregations, you can add pre-defined columns to the table.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Columns for Aggregations</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Auto</strong></p></td><td><p>Columns and their order are determined by the data query. Only available setting when Time Series to Rows, Time Series to Columns, Annotations, or Table is selected.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Avg</strong></p></td><td><p>Averages the recorded data for a given metric.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Min</strong></p></td><td><p>Provides the lowest recorded value for a given metric.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Max</strong></p></td><td><p>Provides the highest recorded value for a given metric.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Total</strong></p></td><td><p>Provides the sum of all values recorded in the table.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Current</strong></p></td><td><p>Shows the current value for a given metric.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Count</strong></p></td><td><p>Provides the total number of data points collected on the metric.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Paging (Options tab)

In the **Options** configuration tab, you can set pagination properties for tables so they donât
become too long and are more manageable.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Paging Properties</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Rows per Page</strong></p></td><td><p>Number of table rows allowed per page.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Scroll</strong></p></td><td><p>Checkbox for making the table scrollable.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Font Size</strong></p></td><td><p>Percentage of the default font size from 80% to 250%.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Column Styles (Options, Data Types, Thresholds, Links)

In the **Column Styles** configuration tab, you can create (**+Add**) and apply rules available for
table columns.

To remove a rule, you need to click it, then find and click the Remove Rule button near the bottom
left of the screen.

### Options (Column Styles tab)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Options</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Apply To Columns Named</strong></p></td><td><p>Identifies the column or columns to which the rule applies. Provide the name or a regular expression (regex) that matches the name of one or more columns in the table. For a regex, use this syntax: <code>/my.*regex/</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Column Header</strong></p></td><td><p>Provides a title for the column. For example, if you add the <code>Avg</code> column to a table that aggregates data, you might change the column name to <code>Average</code>. You can use strings such as <code>$1</code> for a regular expression.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Render Value As Link</strong></p></td><td><p>Treats values in the column as a link that you can configure through the <a href="#settings_link">Link</a> settings.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Link (Column Styles tab)

In the Column Styles configuration tab, you can configure a link from a value to a specified web
page.

Link settings are available only when _Render Value As Link_ is checked.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2"><strong>Link</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>URL</p></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies a URL that you can open when you click a value in the column.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Tooltip</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>For providing a description of the link. This text appears when the end user hovers over the cell with the link. Use the same variables as for URL.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Open In New Tab</p></td><td><p>Displays the web content in a new browser tab when you click on the value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Type</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Type</strong></p></td><td><p>Data type for values in the column. Valid values are Number, String, Date, and Hidden.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Unit</strong></p></td><td><p>Label for the unit of measure to use for the value, such as <code>bytes</code> and <code>megabytes/second</code>. It is important to know the data type for your value before adding this label to the value. A wide variety of units are available.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Decimals</strong></p></td><td><p>The level of precision allowed for a decimal value, for example, <code>2</code> to limit a value of <code>10.12345</code> to <code>10.12</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Thresholds (Column Styles tab)

In the Column Styles configuration tab, you can set thresholds for your data if Type is set to
Number.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2"><strong>Thresholds</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Thresholds</strong></p></td><td><p>Comma-separated values, for example, <code>50000000,100000000</code>. Values between the two values might be treated as warning values. Values below the first value might be treated as acceptable, while values above the second value might be treated as errors. Note that your threshold values must match the unit of measure used for your data (for example, <code>byte</code>) <em>even if the specified Unit is converted to MB or GB in the table</em>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Color Mode</strong></p></td><td><p>Indicates whether to apply the color code to the Value in a cell, to the Cell, to the entire Row, or whether color coding is Disabled.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Color</strong></p></td><td><p>Color used to code values below (left-most color box), between (middle color box), and above (right-most color box) the specified threshold settings. For example, green values might represent OK, yellow a warning, and red an error. You can also invert the color coding.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Configuring Text Panels in a Custom Dashboard

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

[![](../_/img/icons/info.svg)](/monitoring/#billing)

Configure HTML and Markdown text panels in Anypoint Monitoring custom dashboards.

## Before You Begin

Create or navigate to the text panel you plan to configure:

- If your text panel exists, open its configuration view:
  1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open the custom dashboard that contains your text panel.

      For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#view).

  2.  Click the text panelâs ![](_images/more-menu-vert-ellipsis.png) (**More menu**), and select
      **Configure**.

- If the text panel doesnât exist:
  1.  Create the text panel.

      For guidance, see [Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#charts_adding).

  2.  Navigate to its configuration view.

## Create a Text Panel

Create a text panel in a custom dashboard by following the guidance in
[Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#charts_adding).

## Configure a Text Panel

Configure a panel with HTML or Markdown text in a custom dashboard.

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open the custom dashboard that contains your text panel.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](dashboard-custom-config#view).

2.  Click the panelâs ![](_images/more-menu-vert-ellipsis.png) (**More menu**), and select
    **Configure** to open the panelâs **General** configuration tab:
    1.  Provide a title and description.
    2.  Select the **markdown** or **html** mode.
    3.  Provide content in the selected mode.

        For examples and more detailed steps, see:
        - [Add Markdown to a Text Panel](#markdown)
        - [Add HTML to a Text Panel](#html)

3.  Click **Apply Changes**.
4.  Exit the configuration view by clicking the **X** next to the configuration tabs.

## Add Markdown to a Text Panel

This Markdown example shows how your text appears in the UI as you type.

![A text editor that uses Markdown to format text.](_images/text-config.png)

Figure 1. Markdown Example

## Add HTML to a Text Panel

This HTML example creates the heading shown in [Text example](dashboard-custom-config#text_example):

![An HTML example](_images/dashboard-text-config-html.png)
---

# Setting Up Custom Dashboards

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

[![](../_/img/icons/info.svg)](/monitoring/#billing)

In the [US](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/monitoring) and
[EU](https://eu1.anypoint.mulesoft.com/monitoring) clouds, create and configure custom dashboards to
monitor metrics on your Mule apps and APIs.

## View a Custom Dashboard

Find and open a custom dashboard in Anypoint Monitoring.

![Custom dashboards](_images/dashboard-custom-home-hf.png)

To view a custom dashboard:

1.  Navigate to Anypoint Monitoring:

    Click **Monitoring** from the Anypoint Platform home page or from the **Runtimes** menu in the
    header.

2.  In the sidebar, click **Custom Dashboards** to display custom dashboards.
3.  Search for a custom dashboard by name or tag.
4.  Click a name in the list to open the custom dashboard.

    Custom dashboards share common properties:

    ![Custom dashboard example](_images/dashboard-custom-hf.png)

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Configurable name of the custom dashboard</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Configurable title of a row in custom dashboard<div><p>You can add multiple rows to a custom dashboard. Rows can contain multiple charts (also called panels or widgets).</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>Graphs in a custom dashboard row<div><p>You can create graphs, singlestat charts, tables, and text panels for your custom dashboard. For more information, see <a href="#charts_adding">Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard</a>.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td>Lookback window, refresh options, and other dashboard settings:<div><ul><li><p>Zoom in or out.</p></li><li><p>Set a time or date range for the lookback period.</p></li><li><p>Set an autorefresh period, or manually refresh at any time.</p></li><li><p>Favorite your dashboard.</p></li><li><p>View in full screen mode.</p></li><li><p>Duplicate or delete your dashboard.</p></li></ul></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Set Up a Custom Dashboard

Create and configure a custom dashboard in Anypoint Monitoring.

![Custom dashboard configuration](_images/dashboard-custom-config.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Clicking <span><img src="_images/dashboard-custom-config-icon.png" alt="" width="20" height="20"></span> (pencil) opens the dashboard configuration window.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><strong>Dashboard settings</strong> tabs:<div><ul><li><p><strong>General</strong>: For configuring a unique name and description, a time zone (local or UTC), tags, and a tooltip style for your dashboard</p></li><li><p><strong>Rows</strong>: For configuring rows for the charts in your dashboard</p><div><p>See <a href="#rows">Manage Custom Dashboard Rows</a>.</p></div></li><li><p><strong>Metadata</strong>: For viewing dashboard metadata, including creation dates, updates, and versioning</p><div><p>See <a href="#metadata">Get Custom Dashboard Metadata</a>.</p></div></li></ul></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>Charts (also called panels or widgets) you can add to your custom dashboard:<div><ul><li><p><strong>Graph</strong>: Display metrics over time.</p></li><li><p><strong>Singlestat</strong>: Provide a summary statistic (a single total or number) for time-series data, such as a response time or a heap usage statistic.</p></li><li><p><strong>Table</strong>: Display time-series data in a tabular format. You can change cell coloring, date, and value formatting.</p></li><li><p><strong>Text</strong>: Add a panel with textual content in Markdown or HTML.</p><div><p>See <a href="#charts_adding">Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard</a>.</p></div></li></ul></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

Set up a new dashboard:

1.  Navigate to Anypoint Monitoring:

    Click **Monitoring** from the Anypoint Platform home page or from the **Runtimes** menu in the
    header.

2.  In the sidebar, click **Custom Dashboards** to display custom dashboards.
3.  Click **New Dashboard**.
4.  Click ![](_images/dashboard-custom-config-icon.png) to open the dashboard configuration window.
5.  Provide a name and other general dashboard settings.

    ![Dashboard general configurations](_images/dashboard-custom-config-general-hf.png)

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">General Dashboard Settings</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Name for your dashboard.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Description</strong></p></td><td><p>Description of your dashboard.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Tags</strong></p></td><td><p>A short identifier that can help you locate a dashboard in the custom dashboard list.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Timezone</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Most graphs track data over time, so your time options are important. Your options are:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Local browser time</strong>: (Default) Time taken from the system time of the computer that the browser is running on.</p></li><li><p><strong>UTC</strong>: Coordinated Universal Time standard, which uses a 24 hour cycle (for example, <code>19:20</code>) and corresponds to GMT.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Graph Tooltip</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Options include:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Default</strong>: The crosshair cursor appears only in the chart that you hover over.</p></li><li><p><strong>Shared Crosshair</strong>: The crosshair appears on all charts.</p></li><li><p><strong>Shared Tooltip</strong>: The crosshair and the tooltip appear on all charts.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

6.  Add graphs, singlestat charts, tables, or text panels to a row in your custom dashboard.

    For guidance, see [Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard](#charts_adding).

7.  Optionally, click **Rows** to manage the row that contains your chart.

    You can provide a title for a row and make the title visible in the dashboard. You can also
    create additional rows for your charts. See [Manage Custom Dashboard Rows](#rows).

## Add Charts to a Custom Dashboard

Charts are panels (or widgets) you can add to rows in custom dashboards to display metrics on your
Mule apps or APIs.

Add a chart to your dashboard:

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open your custom dashboard.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](#view).

2.  If you need a new row for your chart, scroll down to find and click **Add Row**.

    For row configuration options, see [Manage Custom Dashboard Rows](#rows).

3.  To add a chart to the row, click **Add**.

    ![Custom dashboard widgets](_images/dashboard-custom-widgets.png)

    If chart icons arenât visible in your row:
    1.  Hover over the row to display its ![](_images/more-menu-vert-ellipsis.png) (**More menu**)
        for the row, and click **\+ Add widget**.

        This menu appears at the top of the row.

    2.  Click one of the chart types for your row.

4.  Hover over the chart to display its ![](_images/more-menu-vert-ellipsis.png) (**More menu**),
    and click **Configure**.
5.  To configure the chart in a custom dashboard, see:
    - [Configuring graphs](dashboard-custom-config-graph)
    - [Configuring singlestat charts](dashboard-custom-config-singlestat)
    - [Configuring tables](dashboard-custom-config-table)
    - [Configuring text panels](dashboard-custom-config-text)

## Manage Custom Dashboard Rows

Manage dashboard rows from the dashboard configuration window.

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open your custom dashboard.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](#view).

2.  Click ![](_images/dashboard-custom-config-icon.png) to open the dashboard configuration window.
3.  In **Dashboard Settings**, click **Rows** to configure your dashboard rows.

    ![Dashboard row configuration options](_images/dashboard-custom-config-rows-hf.png)

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Row Configuration Options for Custom Dashboards</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Title</p></td><td><p>Title for your row.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Show Title</p></td><td><p>Make the title visible to end users.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Up Arrow icon</p></td><td><p>Move a row up in the dashboard when there is more than one row in the dashboard.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Down Arrow icon</p></td><td><p>Move a row down when there is more than one row in the dashboard.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Trashcan icon</p></td><td><p>Delete the row and any charts it contains from the dashboard.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Get Custom Dashboard Metadata

Get information about creation dates, updates, and the custom dashboard version.

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open your custom dashboard.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](#view).

2.  Click ![](_images/dashboard-custom-config-icon.png) to open the dashboard configuration window.
3.  In **Dashboard Settings**, click **Metadata** to review your dashboard information.

    ![Dashboard metadata](_images/dashboard-custom-config-metadata-hf.png)

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th colspan="2">Metadata Available for Custom Dashboards</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Last Updated at</strong></p></td><td><p>Date and time of last update to the dashboard.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Last Updated by</strong></p></td><td><p>User who made the last update.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Created at</strong></p></td><td><p>Date and time the dashboard was created.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Created by</strong></p></td><td><p>Username of person who created the dashboard.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Version</strong></p></td><td><p>Version number of the dashboard. The version increments each time you save modifications to the dashboard.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Duplicate a Custom Dashboard

Create a copy of a dashboard. The copy requires a unique name but retains the configuration and all
charts. You can modify that configuration as needed.

1.  From **Custom Dashboards**, open a custom dashboard.

    For guidance, see [View a Custom Dashboard](#view).
